title: Script Structure and Observability — Quick Check

questions.q: List the four steps in the canonical main() execution sequence and explain why order matters.
questions.a: parse → log → validate → dispatch. Order matters because logging must be configured before any log call is made, and validation must run before dispatch so the script fails fast with a clear error rather than deep inside business logic.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the `if __name__ == '__main__'` guard, and what capability does it unlock?
questions.a: It prevents top-level code from running when the file is imported as a module. This makes the script importable, which in turn makes it testable — pytest can import and call main() directly without spawning a subprocess.

questions.q: Why should scripts use module-level loggers created with `logging.getLogger(__name__)` rather than the root logger?
questions.a: Module-level loggers carry the file's qualified name, making log messages self-identifying. They also integrate into the logging hierarchy, so callers can silence or redirect a specific module's output without affecting the whole application.

questions.q: How would you expose log verbosity as a CLI argument, and what Python logging level would you map to `-v`?
questions.a: Add a `--log-level` or `-v` flag via argparse, then call `logging.basicConfig(level=getattr(logging, args.log_level.upper()))` in main(). A single `-v` conventionally maps to `logging.DEBUG`.
